file_name: Medela et al. - 2024 - The utility and reliability of a deep learning algorithm as a diagnosis support tool in head & neck.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a CE-certified medical device leveraging deep learning (specifically a vision transformer, ViT-base) and computer vision algorithms to classify non-melanoma skin cancers (BCC, cSCC) and benign lesions in a dermatology/head & neck context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes a prospective, longitudinal, non-randomized study involving 135 patients to evaluate the performance of a deep learning-based medical device for diagnosing head and neck non-melanoma skin cancers. It presents original experimental data, including patient demographics, histological confirmation, and diagnostic accuracy metrics (top-k accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC-ROC) comparing the device's performance on a curated H&N dataset versus a broader MDD dataset.

title: The utility and reliability of a deep learning algorithm as a diagnosis support tool in head & neck non-melanoma skin malignancies
brief_summary: This prospective, longitudinal study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a CE-certified deep learning medical device, utilizing a vision transformer (ViT-base), for classifying head and neck non-melanoma skin cancers. The device was tested on a curated dataset of 135 patients with histologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma, cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, and benign lesions, alongside a broader MDD dataset. Evaluation metrics included top-k accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC-ROC to assess the tool's ability to distinguish malignant from benign pathologies. The results demonstrated that the device achieved remarkable performance, correctly identifying malignant lesions within the top-5 most likely diagnoses in over 90% of cases. The study highlights the potential utility of this AI-driven tool as a diagnostic aid in primary care, dermatology, and head & neck surgery clinics for early detection and triage.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant pathology; 54 cases in the H&N dataset; also present in the MDD dataset (952 images).
Malignant pathology; 54 cases in the H&N dataset; also present in the MDD dataset (363 images).
Benign pathology; 14 cases in the H&N dataset; also present in the MDD dataset (1760 images).
Benign pathology; 11 cases in the H&N dataset; also present in the MDD dataset (18 images).
Benign pathology; 2 cases in the H&N dataset; also present in the MDD dataset (728 images).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Basal cell carcinoma, Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, Actinic Cheilitis, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis) and binary malignancy status (malignant vs. benign).
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates a deep learning algorithm (Vision Transformer) for classifying specific non-melanoma skin cancer types and benign lesions. Performance is measured using top-k accuracy (top-1, top-3, top-5), class-wise sensitivity, and specificity. The model also performs binary classification for malignancy estimation, assessed via ROC curves and AUC-ROC scores.

datasets_used.name: H&N Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated, prospective dataset of images from patients referred to the Head & Neck clinic for suspicious skin lesions. Images were captured using an Apple iPhone 13 with standardized lighting and distance (10 cm), manually cropped to the lesion, and histologically confirmed. Managed exclusively by the senior author.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the CE-certified medical device in a real clinical scenario (external validation of the device's generalization to this specific curated cohort).

datasets_used.name: MDD Dataset (Medical Device Dataset)
datasets_used.description: The dataset used to train and evaluate the CE-certified medical device (Legit.Health). It is a compilation of over 200,000 images from diverse public and private dermatological sources (clinical and dermatoscopic), some labeled by dermatologists or verified by pathologists. The study utilized a specific subset of this dataset's test set for comparison.
classes_used: 239 different skin diseases and families of skin pathologies

use.training: The full MDD dataset (train and validation splits) was used for the deep learning training procedure of the ViT-base model.
use.validation: The validation split of the MDD dataset was used during the training procedure.
use.testing: A subset of the MDD test set (3,821 images corresponding to the 5 H&N pathologies) was used for external validation/comparison of the medical device's performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the medical device by measuring the proportion of cases where the correct diagnosis was included in the top-k most likely predictions.
To assess the model's ability to correctly identify positive and negative cases for each specific pathology within the top-5 predictions, using a threshold of 0.01 to suppress unlikely pathologies.

To visualize and evaluate the trade-off between true positive rate (sensitivity) and false positive rate (1-specificity) for the binary classification of malignant versus benign lesions across varying malignancy thresholds.
To quantify the overall diagnostic performance of the malignancy estimation assessment, representing the integration of the ROC curve.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics, accuracy metrics (top-k accuracy, sensitivity, specificity), and ROC/AUC-ROC analysis.

H&N Dataset.description: Curated dataset of 135 patients with histologically confirmed lesions from a Head & Neck clinic.
H&N Dataset.notes: The device identified malignant lesions (BCC and cSCC) within the top-5 most likely diagnoses in above 90% of cases. Benign lesions were also consistently identified correctly within the top-5. The H&N dataset showed higher overall performance on top-3 and top-5 metrics compared to the MDD dataset.

MDD Dataset.description: Subset of the Medical Device Dataset test set containing 3,821 images related to the 5 skin pathologies present in the H&N dataset.
MDD Dataset.notes: The MDD dataset exhibited lower top-5 class-wise sensitivity compared to the H&N dataset, but higher specificity for most classes. The discrepancy in performance between datasets is attributed to the curated nature of the H&N dataset versus the diverse sources of the MDD dataset.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained end-to-end on the Medical Device Dataset (MDD), a compilation of over 200,000 images from public and private dermatological sources (clinical and dermatoscopic images), some labeled by dermatologists and/or verified by pathologists.

architecture_details.task: Classification of 239 different skin diseases and families of skin pathologies
architecture_details.implementation: CE-certified medical device (Legit.Health) leveraging computer vision algorithms

training_details.splits: Split into train, validation, and test sets; train and validation sets used for deep learning training procedure

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Probabilities for top-k predictions (top-1, top-3, top-5)
classification_or_output_head.thresholding: Used a minimum threshold of 0.01 for top-5 class-wise sensitivity/specificity calculations to suppress unlikely pathologies

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of images to focus on the captured lesion
Standardized image acquisition with consistent camera settings (Apple iPhone 13, 12 megapixels, regular mode) and lighting conditions (regular light from the clinic)
Exclusion of mobile phone zoom and flash during capture
Avoidance of surgical ink markers on the skin
conclusions: The deep learning-based medical device (Legit.Health, utilizing a Vision Transformer) demonstrated high effectiveness as a diagnostic aid for head and neck non-melanoma skin cancers, specifically basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC).

The device achieved remarkable performance in identifying malignant lesions within the top-5 most likely diagnoses in over 90% of cases in the curated H&N dataset, underscoring its potential utility for early diagnosis and treatment.
Benign lesions were also consistently identified correctly within the top-5 most likely pathologies.
The device achieved an AUC-ROC score of 0.93 for malignancy estimation on the H&N dataset and 0.92 on the MDD dataset, indicating excellent balance between sensitivity and specificity.

The H&N dataset showed higher overall performance on top-3 and top-5 metrics compared to the MDD dataset, which the authors attribute to the curated nature of the H&N dataset versus the diverse sources of the MDD dataset.

The lower specificity in the H&N dataset compared to the MDD dataset is attributed to visual similarities between pathologies (e.g., seborrheic keratosis and squamous cell carcinoma), which led to the simultaneous inclusion of both in the top-5 predictions, thereby increasing sensitivity but diminishing specificity.

The study highlights the potential value of this AI-driven tool for use in primary care clinics, dermatology, and head & neck surgery clinics, as well as for patients with suspicious lesions as a self-exploration tool.
Further research is needed to elucidate the role of this technology in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study has a small sample size (n=135), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
There is a lack of comparison with human dermatologists or other clinicians, preventing an assessment of non-inferiority or superiority against expert human diagnosis.

The H&N dataset was meticulously curated with standardized imaging protocols (fixed distance, lighting, device) managed by senior authors, which may introduce selection bias and overestimate performance compared to real-world, uncontrolled clinical settings.
The model's performance on the diverse MDD dataset was lower than on the curated H&N dataset, highlighting potential generalization issues when applied to varied, uncurated clinical data.

Specificity was notably lower for Basal Cell Carcinoma (0.56) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (0.58) in the H&N dataset, attributed to visual similarities with benign lesions like seborrheic keratosis leading to simultaneous inclusion in top-5 predictions.
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the diagnostic metrics.

Potential data leakage or bias exists as the images were manually cropped by the senior author and captured under controlled conditions, differing from typical patient-generated or varied clinical images.

The device is a CE-certified medical device (Legit.Health) using a Vision Transformer (ViT-base) trained on a large, diverse dataset (MDD), but its evaluation in this specific study was limited to a small, curated cohort.
